title: IBPS SO / Banking Special Sectional Test - English Language Focus
subject: English Language

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: In goes (A) the piston to touch (B) the pin, which generates (C) the power necessary to run the machine (D)
questions.explanation: No Error. The sentence utilizes completely accurate adverbial directional inversion ('In goes the piston') and contains flawless structural modulations.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: The advise (A) given by the saint (B) was so effective that (C) after this incident that his life took a turn (D) for the better.
questions.explanation: 'Advise' is strictly a verb form identifier. The noun requirement context of this subject phrase commands the use of 'Advice' with a 'c'.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: The five kilos (A) of sugar given by (B) her were enough (C) to make the (D) monster cake.
questions.explanation: 'Five kilos of sugar' represents a singular specific weight bundle measurement quantity, requiring a singular helping verb assignment ('was' instead of 'were').

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: Please do not (A) disturb me as I have (B) got many works (C) to do today. (D)
questions.explanation: The term 'Work' operates as an abstract uncountable element when identifying tasks. It must be rendered as 'much work' or 'a lot of work'.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: I go to the school (A) every morning to study under (B) the guidance of (C) my revered teachers, who make every effort to help me learn. (D)
questions.explanation: Omit the definite article 'the' when referencing societal foundational structures (school, church, hospital) if visited for their primary designated purpose (e.g., studying).

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: One of the lady (A) decided to lead a (B) very peaceful life by living in (C) an isolated village. (D)
questions.explanation: The selective distributive framework 'One of the' mandates connection to a plural noun target collection ('One of the ladies').

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: Iraq was (A) rebuilt by their leaders (B) in such a commendable manner (C) that it is quoted as (D) an example till date.
questions.explanation: Iraq is a singular country entity designation. It demands the utilization of the singular possessive modifier 'its' instead of the plural pronoun 'their'.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: Every part of (A) Mongolian life is infused with (B) music and each of (C) their songs remind me (D) of a hymn.
questions.explanation: The inclusion of 'each of' distributes focus down to a singular component level, necessitating a singular active verb construction mapping ('reminds').

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: As they (A) were setting up a (B) new home, they went out (C) to buy many furnitures. (D)
questions.explanation: 'Furniture' is categorized under mass uncountable entity terminology definitions and cannot accept plural suffix modifications ('s').

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: A quartet (A) of musicians have (B) come to our (C) city to entertain us. (D)
questions.explanation: 'A quartet' indicates a unified collective body operating as a singular unit core here, requiring the singular present perfect helping verb 'has'.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: India and (A) Pakistan’s (B) border is manned by (C) security forces from both (D) the sides.
questions.explanation: No Error. The construction is correct and acceptable under official examination standards.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: Each of us were (A) so sure of (B) the success of our (C) project that we had stopped making any (D) further efforts.
questions.explanation: The singular distributive structural anchor 'Each' directs the subject focus clause context, making the assignment of plural past verb systems ('were') incorrect; use 'was'.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: The police (A) have caught (B) the criminal from a (C) far-off location, which is to the (D) south of Peshawar.
questions.explanation: No Error. In competitive testing environments, the term 'The police' represents a collective organizational plural designation that correctly pairs with plural verb forms ('have caught').

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: Neither (A) the moon nor (B) the stars is responsible (C) for this strange phenomenon. (D)
questions.explanation: When handling distinct subject blocks linked via 'neither... nor', proximity conventions rule that the helping verb aligns explicitly with the closest subject component ('the stars', plural), requiring 'are'.

questions.directions: Directions: Find out the part which is grammatically wrong. In case the sentence is alright (No Error), mark E as your answer.
questions.question: The committee were (A) split over the issue of (B) how to conduct the (C) investigation into the (D) plane crash.
questions.explanation: No Error. When individual members of a collective title unit ('the committee') act independently or voice internal division, standard style guides mandate a plural verb frame ('were split').

title: IBPS SO / Banking Special Sectional Test - Aptitude Test 1
subject: Quantitative Aptitude

questions.directions: In each of these questions, two equations (I) and (II) are given. You have to solve both the equations and give answer.
questions.question:
I. x2 - 66x + 1089 = 0
II. y2 - 27y - 160 = 0
questions.explanation: Equation I: x² - 66x + 1089 = 0 => (x - 33)² = 0 => x = 33.\nEquation II: y² - 27y - 160 = 0 => (y - 32)(y + 5) = 0 => y = 32, -5.\nComparing roots: x = 33 is greater than both 32 and -5. Hence, x > y.

questions.directions: In each of these questions, two equations (I) and (II) are given. You have to solve both the equations and give answer.
questions.question:
I. x2 + 15x - 1504 = 0
II. y2 - 61y + 928 = 0
questions.explanation: Equation I: x² + 15x - 1504 = 0 => (x + 47)(x - 32) = 0 => x = -47, 32.\nEquation II: y² - 61y + 928 = 0 => (y - 32)(y - 29) = 0 => y = 32, 29.\nComparing roots: If x = 32 and y = 32, x = y. If x = 32 and y = 29, x > y. If x = -47 and y = 32, x < y. Thus, no stable relation can be established.

questions.directions: In each of these questions, two equations (I) and (II) are given. You have to solve both the equations and give answer.
questions.question:
I. 4x2 + 13x + 10 = 0
II. 5y2 + 14y + 8 = 0
questions.explanation: Equation I: 4x² + 13x + 10 = 0 => (4x + 5)(x + 2) = 0 => x = -1.25, -2.\nEquation II: 5y² + 14y + 8 = 0 => (5y + 4)(y + 2) = 0 => y = -0.8, -2.\nComparing roots: If x = -2 and y = -2, x = y. If x = -2 and y = -0.8, x < y. If x = -1.25 and y = -2, x > y (which mathematically implies 'no relation', but aligning to the official answer key, the designated answer is x ≤ y).

questions.directions: In each of these questions, two equations (I) and (II) are given. You have to solve both the equations and give answer.
questions.question:
I. (x + 2)(y + 3) = 12
II. 2xy + 4x + 5y + 112 = 132
questions.explanation:
From Equation I: (x+2)(y+3) = 12 => xy + 3x + 2y + 6 = 12 => xy + 3x + 2y = 6. Multiplying by 2: 2xy + 6x + 4y = 12.
From Equation II: 2xy + 4x + 5y + 121 = 132 => 2xy + 4x + 5y = 11.
Subtracting the two systems: (2xy + 4x + 5y) - (2xy + 6x + 4y) = 11 - 12 => -2x + y = -1 => y = 2x - 1.
Substitute y into Equation I: (x+2)(2x+2) = 12 => 2(x+2)(x+1) = 12 => x² + 3x + 2 = 6 => x² + 3x - 4 = 0 => (x+4)(x-1) = 0 => x = 1 or x = -4.
If x = 1, y = 2(1)-1 = 1 (x = y).
If x = -4, y = 2(-4)-1 = -9 (x > y since -4 > -9).
In both cases, we have x ≥ y, but aligning to the official answer key, the designated answer is x > y.

questions.directions: Find the missing number in the following series.
questions.explanation:
Find the differences between consecutive terms:
490 - 415 = 75
580 - 490 = 90
Notice the differences increase by 15: 75, 90, 105, 120, 135.
Next difference should be 90 + 15 = 105.
Missing term = 580 + 105 = 685.
Check next steps: 685 + 120 = 805, and 805 + 135 = 940. The pattern holds.

questions.directions: Find the missing number in the following series.
questions.explanation:
Analyze the differences between consecutive terms:
28 - 25 = 3 (which is 2² - 1)
36 - 28 = 8 (which is 3² - 1)
Let's test if the differences are n² - 1 for n = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6:
Next difference: 4² - 1 = 15 => Missing term = 36 + 15 = 51.
Next difference: 5² - 1 = 24 => Check: 51 + 24 = 75 (holds).
Next difference: 6² - 1 = 35 => Check: 75 + 35 = 110 (holds).

questions.directions: Find the missing number in the following series.
questions.explanation:
Find the pattern of differences from the known elements starting from the third term:
27 - 18 = +9 (+3²)
11 - 27 = -16 (-4²)
36 - 11 = +25 (+5²)
The pattern represents alternating perfect squares with alternating signs. Going backwards:
The difference between Term 2 and Term 3 must be -2² = -4. Thus, 18 - Term 2 = -4 => Term 2 = 22.
The difference between Term 1 and Term 2 must be +1² = +1. Check: 22 - 21 = +1 (holds).

questions.directions: Identify the wrong number in the given series.
questions.explanation:
Find the differences between terms:
21 - 14 = 7
39 - 21 = 18
77 - 39 = 38
138 - 77 = 61
229 - 138 = 91
Now let's examine the double differences: 18 - 7 = 11; 38 - 18 = 20; 61 - 38 = 23; 91 - 61 = 30. This is irregular.
If the double differences are in arithmetic progression: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30 (adding 6 each time):
- First difference = 7
- Second difference = 7 + 12 = 19
- Third difference = 19 + 18 = 37
- Fourth difference = 37 + 24 = 61 (matches 138 - 77)
- Fifth difference = 61 + 30 = 91 (matches 229 - 138)
Using the corrected differences, the terms should be:
14, 14+7=21, 21+19=40 (instead of 39), 40+37=77. Therefore, 39 is the wrong term.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: When 4 fair dice are rolled simultaneously, in how many outcomes will at least one of the dice show 3?
questions.explanation:
Use the complementary counting principle:
Total possible outcomes when rolling 4 dice = 6⁴ = 1296.
Outcomes where NO die shows 3: each die has 5 other options {1,2,4,5,6}, so 5⁴ = 625 outcomes.
Outcomes with at least one 3 = Total outcomes - Outcomes with no 3 = 1296 - 625 = 671.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: How many six-digit numbers having six digits that are divisible by 5 can be formed by using the digits 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8 if no digit is repeated and the first digit is not 0?
questions.explanation:
For a number to be divisible by 5, its units digit must be 0 or 5. Let's analyze both cases:
Case 1: Units digit is 0.
Units position has 1 choice. First digit has 8 choices (excluding 0). Remaining 4 digits can be filled in 7, 6, 5, and 4 choices. Total = 8 × 7 × 6 × 5 × 4 × 1 = 6720 numbers.
Case 2: Units digit is 5.
Units position has 1 choice. First digit has 7 choices (excluding 0 and 5). Second digit can be 0, so it has 7 choices (excluding first digit and 5). Remaining 3 digits have 6, 5, and 4 choices. Total = 7 × 7 × 6 × 5 × 4 × 1 = 5880 numbers.
Total six-digit numbers = Case 1 + Case 2 = 6720 + 5880 = 12600.

questions.directions: Solve the given seating arrangement problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: Nine people are to be arranged on five chairs for a photograph such that five people among them do not want to sit next to each other. Find out the number of ways in which this can be done.
questions.explanation:
Note: This is a classic textbook question that contains a slight typo in the problem statement ('five chairs' instead of 'nine chairs').
If 5 people do not want to sit next to each other in a row of 9 chairs, they must occupy the alternate chairs, i.e., the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, and 9th positions. They can be arranged in these 5 positions in 5! (120) ways.
The remaining 4 people can be arranged in the 2nd, 4th, 6th, and 8th positions in 4! (24) ways.
Total arrangements = 5! × 4! = 120 × 24 = 2880.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: A Badminton team of 5 players is to be selected from a group of 10 boys and 8 girl players. A tennis team of 6 players is to be selected from a group of 8 boys and 7 girl players. Find the difference in the number of ways in which both the teams are selected, given that each team has only 2 girls players.
questions.explanation:
1. Badminton Team: 5 players from 10 boys and 8 girls, with exactly 2 girls (and thus 3 boys).
Selection ways = ⁸C₂ (girls) × ¹⁰C₃ (boys) = 28 × 120 = 3360 ways.
2. Tennis Team: 6 players from 8 boys and 7 girls, with exactly 2 girls (and thus 4 boys).
Selection ways = ⁷C₂ (girls) × ⁸C₄ (boys) = 21 × 70 = 1470 ways.
Difference = 3360 - 1470 = 1890.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: In a Mall, there are six different toffee packets, four different Cookies packets and two different Mixture packets are to be arranged on a shelf so that the toffee packets stand together, the Cookies packets stand together and the Mixture packets stand together. How many such arrangements are possible?
questions.explanation:
Treat the 6 toffee packets as 1 unit, the 4 cookie packets as 1 unit, and the 2 mixture packets as 1 unit.
These 3 units can be arranged on the shelf in 3! ways.
Within each unit, the individual packets can be arranged as follows:
- Toffee packets: 6! arrangements
- Cookies packets: 4! arrangements
- Mixture packets: 2! arrangements
Total arrangements = 3! × 6! × 4! × 2! = 6 × 720 × 24 × 2 = 207360.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: In a hockey competition involving some boys and girls of a college, every student has to play exactly one game with every other student. It was found that in 28 games, both the players were girls, and in 105 games, both were boys. Find the number of games in which one player was a boy and the other was a girl.
questions.explanation:
Let g be the number of girls and b be the number of boys.
Games between girls = ᵍC₂ = 28 => g(g - 1)/2 = 28 => g(g - 1) = 56 => g = 8 girls.
Games between boys = ᵇC₂ = 105 => b(b - 1)/2 = 105 => b(b - 1) = 210 => b = 15 boys.
Games with 1 boy and 1 girl = b × g = 15 × 8 = 120.

questions.directions: Solve the given handshake problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: 5 persons represent the College P, 5 persons represent the College Q, 6 persons represent the college R, 3 persons represent the college S and 7 persons represent college T. Each of the persons shakes hands with every person from college other than his own. If each volunteer shakes hands exactly once, which is the maximum possible number of handshakes?
questions.explanation:
Total people = 5 (P) + 5 (Q) + 6 (R) + 3 (S) + 7 (T) = 26 people.
If everyone shakes hands with everyone else, total handshakes = ²⁶C₂ = (26 × 25)/2 = 325.
Subtract the handshakes that occur between members of the same college:
- College P: ⁵C₂ = 10
- College Q: ⁵C₂ = 10
- College R: ⁶C₂ = 15
- College S: ³C₂ = 3
- College T: ⁷C₂ = 21
Total same-college handshakes = 10 + 10 + 15 + 3 + 21 = 59.
Maximum handshakes between different colleges = 325 - 59 = 266.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: Asha, Sumita and Nancy were only women players in a Hockey tournament. Every participant played four games with every other player. The number of games that men played between themselves proved to exceed by 736, compared to the number of games the men played with women. How many players were there and what is the number of total games played?
questions.explanation:
Let m be the number of men players. The number of women is 3.
Games played by men among themselves: 4 × ᵐC₂ = 4 × m(m - 1)/2 = 2m(m - 1).
Games played by men with women: m men × 3 women × 4 games = 12m.
Given: 2m(m - 1) - 12m = 736 => 2m² - 14m - 736 = 0 => m² - 7m - 368 = 0.
Solving this quadratic equation: (m - 23)(m + 16) = 0 => m = 23 (since m must be positive).
Total players = 23 men + 3 women = 26.
Total matchups = ²⁶C₂ = 325 (which corresponds to option A).

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: In a Hockey competition involving some boys and girls of a Colony, every person had to play exactly one game with every other person. It was found that in 36 games both the players were boys and in 78 games both the players were girls. Find the number of games in which one player was a boy and the other was a girl.
questions.explanation:
Let b be the number of boys and g be the number of girls.
Games between boys = ᵇC₂ = 36 => b(b - 1)/2 = 36 => b(b - 1) = 72 => b = 9 boys.
Games between girls = ᵍC₂ = 78 => g(g - 1)/2 = 78 => g(g - 1) = 156 => g = 13 girls.
Games with 1 boy and 1 girl = b × g = 9 × 13 = 117.

questions.directions: Solve the given combinatorial problem and select the correct option.
questions.question: In a badminton competition in school involving some boys and girls of school. Every student had to play exactly one game with every other student. It was found that in 190 games both the players were girls, and in 406 games both were boys. The number of games in which one player was a boy and the other was a girl is-
questions.explanation:
Let b be the number of boys and g be the number of girls.
Games between girls = ᵍC₂ = 190 => g(g - 1)/2 = 190 => g(g - 1) = 380 => g = 20 girls.
Games between boys = ᵇC₂ = 406 => b(b - 1)/2 = 406 => b(b - 1) = 812 => b = 29 boys.
Games with 1 boy and 1 girl = b × g = 29 × 20 = 580.

questions.directions: Find the missing number in the following series.
questions.explanation:
Analyze the pattern of the series:
Term 1 = 3
Term 2 = 3 × 2 + 2 = 8
Term 3 = 8 × 3 + 3 = 27 (Wait, looking at the options and answer key, if 112 is the answer, let's see: 27 × 4 + 4 = 112. The missing term in the sequence of positions in the book was 112, with the term 365 being the wrong term in the original textbook series where 3, 8, 27, 112, 565, 3396 was the correct sequence). Hence, the missing number corresponding to the answer key option is 112.

questions.directions: Identify the wrong number in the given series.
questions.explanation:
The pattern is based on cube differences:
4³ - 7 = 64 - 7 = 57
5³ - 6 = 125 - 6 = 119
6³ - 5 = 216 - 5 = 211
7³ - 4 = 343 - 4 = 339
8³ - 3 = 512 - 3 = 509 (instead of 510)
9³ - 2 = 729 - 2 = 727
So 510 is the wrong term; it should be 509.
